title: Default Boxes & Aspect Ratios
concept: SSD uses default boxes of varying aspect ratios across different layer resolutions as templates to locate objects.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What layers in SSD are best suited for detecting small objects?
exercises.explain: Correct! Earlier layers retain fine-grained spatial details, making them ideal for small objects.

cards.title: The Box Stencils
cards.body: How does SSD know what shape an object is before drawing a box around it? Instead of guessing from scratch, SSD uses a set of preset coordinate guides called 'default boxes'.

cards.title: Default Boxes & Aspect Ratios
cards.body: Default Boxes are preset template bounding boxes tiled across every grid position of a feature map. Each cell predicts offsets relative to multiple default boxes of different aspect ratios (e.g. 1:1, 2:1, 1:2).

cards.title: A Stencil Kit
cards.body: Imagine you are drawing shapes. Instead of drawing a freehand box, you use a stencil kit with a pre-cut square (1:1), a horizontal rectangle (2:1), and a vertical strip (1:2). You place the stencil over the target and adjust it slightly.

cards.title: Matching the Object
cards.body: When SSD detects a standing human, it matches the object to a tall, narrow default box (1:2 aspect ratio). For a car, it matches to a wide default box (2:1 aspect ratio), regressing the exact box border offsets from there.

cards.title: Identical Box Scales?
cards.body: You might think the default boxes are the same size across all layers. In fact, SSD scales box sizes: earlier high-resolution layers get small default boxes, while later low-resolution layers get large default boxes.

cards.title: Default Box Shapes
cards.body: How default boxes map to grid cells in SSD:

left.text:
Ideal for vertical objects
e.g. Pedestrians, poles
Regresses vertical offset

right.text:
Ideal for horizontal objects
e.g. Vehicles, boxes
Regresses horizontal offset

exercises.prompt: What is the purpose of default boxes in SSD?

options.text: They serve as predefined shape templates that the network refines by predicting offsets
They crop the input image into small boxes
They define the size of the convolution kernels
They are used to delete duplicate detections

exercises.explain: Correct! Default boxes act as templates of different aspect ratios for predictions.

exercises.prompt: True or False: SSD uses larger default boxes in the deeper (low-resolution) layers of the network.
exercises.explain: True. Deeper layers capture large objects, so SSD uses larger default box scales in those layers.

exercises.sentence: SSD default boxes are defined with different ___ ratios to match both tall and wide objects.
exercises.explain: Aspect ratios (like 1:1, 2:1) help match default boxes to different object shapes.

exercises.prompt: If a default box has a 1:2 aspect ratio, what kind of object is it best suited to detect?

options.text: A tall, narrow object like a pedestrian
A wide, flat object like a car
A square object like a stop sign

exercises.explain: Correct! An aspect ratio of 1:2 means the box is twice as tall as it is wide, perfect for pedestrians.

exercises.prompt: You are detecting wide trucks and tall light poles in an image. How does SSD use default boxes to handle both classes in the same grid cell?

options.text: Each grid cell evaluates multiple default boxes, matching the truck to a wide (2:1) stencil and the pole to a tall (1:2) stencil.
It runs the model twice: once for trucks and once for poles.
It dynamically changes the shape of the grid cell itself.
It ignores the aspect ratio and uses only squares.

exercises.explain: Exactly! Multiple default boxes per cell allow the network to handle different object shapes at the exact same location.
